abstract.text: Downloads, decodes, processes, and caches images.

inlineContent.text: The pipeline is the central component of Nuke. It orchestrates a graph of
tasks - fetching data, decoding, processing, and decompressing images -
while automatically coalescing duplicate work, respecting priorities, and

code: let image = try await ImagePipeline.shared.image(for: url)

inlineContent.text: pipeline and create a custom one later if needed. To create a custom pipeline, you can use a convenience
. You can provide custom caches, data loaders, add support for new image formats, and more.
The pipeline has two cache layers: memory cache and disk cache. By default, only the memory cache is enabled. For caching data persistently, it relies on system
. There are advantages to enabling a custom disk cache. You can learn more in

code: let image = try await ImagePipeline.shared.image(for: url)

inlineContent.text: to fetch the image. You can use
to cancel the request, change the priority of the running task, and observe its progress.
The recommended way to load images with
is by using Async/Await API. But the pipeline also has API that works with closures and Combine publishers.
The pipeline has two cache layers: memory cache and disk cache.

stores images prepared for display in memory. It uses a fraction of available RAM and automatically removes most of the cached images when the app goes to the background or receives a memory pressure warning.
For caching data persistently, by default, Nuke uses
with an increased capacity. One of its advantages is HTTP
and provides more control. For example, it can be configured to store processed images using
. The downside is that without HTTP
, the images never get validated and if the URL content changes, the app will continue showing stale data.
To learn more about caching, see
The pipeline avoids doing any duplicated work when loading images. Let’s take two requests with the same URL but different processors as an example:

code: async let first = pipeline.image(for: ImageRequest(url: url, processors: [
async let second = pipeline.image(for: ImageRequest(url: url, processors: [

inlineContent.text: The pipeline will load the data only once, resize the image once and blur it only once. There is no duplicated work done. The work only gets canceled when all the registered requests are, and the priority is based on the highest priority of the registered requests.
If progressive decoding is enabled, the pipeline attempts to produce previews as data arrives. The behavior is controlled by
for all other formats (baseline JPEGs, PNGs, etc.). This means only formats that benefit from incremental rendering produce previews by default.
to produce a new preview as more data arrives. For JPEGs with large EXIF headers where incremental decoding fails, the decoder automatically falls back to generating a thumbnail.
— Extracts the embedded EXIF thumbnail (if any), then stops.
The pipeline throttles progressive decoding attempts using
(default: 0.5s). When data arrives faster than this interval, intermediate chunks are skipped. This prevents excessive decoding work on fast connections.

Every preview goes through the same processing and decompression phases as the final image. If a stage can’t keep up, the pipeline waits for the current operation to finish before starting the next one. All outstanding progressive operations are canceled when the data is fully downloaded.

Enables task coalescing. When enabled, the pipeline avoids duplicated
work when loading images. A task is only cancelled when all requests
associated with it are cancelled. The pipeline also automatically
manages the priority of the deduplicated work.
